claim: The alloy Fe-26Al-4Nb-1C is three-phase, containing the Laves phase (Fe, Al)2Nb besides NbC carbides in the Fe3Al matrix.
evidence_text: An exception is the alloy Fe–26Al–4Nb–1C which was found to be three-phase with the Laves phase (Fe, Al)2Nb besides NbC carbides in the Fe3Al matrix.

claim: Compression test specimens had dimensions of 6 × 6 × 10 mm³.
evidence_text: The dimensions of compression samples were (6 × 6 × 10) mm^3.

claim: Tensile test specimens had a flat cross-section of 3.5 × 5 mm² with a gauge length of 15 mm.
evidence_text: For tensile tests, flat samples of (3.5 × 5) mm² cross-section with 15 mm gauge length were used.

claim: All mechanical tests were carried out at a deformation rate of 10⁻⁴ s⁻¹.
evidence_text: All mechanical tests were carried out at a deformation rate of 10^{-4} s^{-1}

claim: Fe-26Al-4Nb-1C has polygonal grains of size 60 μm, and contains NbC precipitates with cube-like morphology of size 1.8 μm and (Fe,Al)2Nb Laves phase with plate-like morphology of size 2×5 μm.
Compression samples had dimensions of 6×6×10 mm³, and tensile samples had a flat cross-section of 3.5×5 mm² with 15 mm gauge length, all cut along the casting direction with surfaces ground.
evidence_text: The dimensions of compression samples were 6×6×10 mm^3. For tensile tests, flat samples of 3.5×5 mm^2 cross-section with 15 mm gauge length were used. All samples were cut in the direction of the longitudinal axis of the ingot (casting direction). The surfaces of the specimen were ground.

claim: All mechanical tests were performed at a deformation rate of 10⁻⁴ s⁻¹.
evidence_text: All mechanical tests were carried out at a deformation rate of 10⁻⁴ s⁻¹

claim: Binary Fe–15Al alloy has a room-temperature yield stress in compression of 356 MPa, yield stress in tension of 344 MPa, total elongation of 16%, Brinell hardness of 159 HB, and fractures by transgranular cleavage (Table 2).

Fe–15Al–2Ti–1C alloy exhibits a room-temperature yield stress in compression of 485 MPa, yield stress in tension of 420 MPa, total elongation of 3.3%, Brinell hardness of 197 HB, and fractures by transgranular cleavage (Table 2).

Fe–15Al–2V–1C alloy has a room-temperature yield stress in compression of 396 MPa, yield stress in tension of 374 MPa, total elongation of 0.6%, Brinell hardness of 191 HB, and fractures by transgranular cleavage (Table 2).

Fe–15Al–2Nb–1C alloy achieves a room-temperature yield stress in compression of 827 MPa and Brinell hardness of 309 HB; no tensile data were obtained, and the fracture mode is transgranular cleavage (Table 2).

Fe–15Al–2Ta–1C alloy shows a room-temperature yield stress in compression of 868 MPa and Brinell hardness of 291 HB; no tensile data were obtained, and the fracture mode is transgranular cleavage (Table 2).

Binary Fe–26Al alloy has a room-temperature yield stress in compression of 644 MPa, yield stress in tension of 475 MPa, total elongation of 0.4%, Brinell hardness of 260 HB, and fractures by transgranular cleavage (Table 2).

Fe–26Al–2Ti–1C alloy exhibits a room-temperature yield stress in compression of 447 MPa, yield stress in tension of 342 MPa, total elongation of 0.7%, Brinell hardness of 251 HB, and mixed intergranular decohesion plus transgranular cleavage fracture (IGD+TGC) (Table 2).
Fe–26Al–4Ti–1C alloy has a room-temperature yield stress in compression of 454 MPa, Brinell hardness of 229 HB, no recorded tensile data, and fractures by mixed IGD+TGC (Table 2).

Fe–26Al–2V–1C alloy shows a room-temperature yield stress in compression of 544 MPa, yield stress in tension of 350 MPa, total elongation of 0.5%, Brinell hardness of 286 HB, and transgranular cleavage fracture (Table 2).

Fe–26Al–2Nb–1C alloy achieves a room-temperature yield stress in compression of 601 MPa, yield stress in tension of 427 MPa, total elongation of 1.4%, Brinell hardness of 275 HB, and transgranular cleavage fracture (Table 2).
Fe–26Al–4Nb–1C alloy has a room-temperature yield stress in compression of 792 MPa, Brinell hardness of 303 HB, no tensile data recorded, and mixed IGD+TGC fracture (Table 2).
Fe–26Al–2Ta–1C alloy shows a room-temperature yield stress in compression of 594 MPa, Brinell hardness of 288 HB, no tensile data recorded, and mixed IGD+TGC fracture (Table 2).

Fe–26Al–(2 or 4)M–1C alloys display very high strengthening with nearly constant hardening rates in room-temperature compression, transitioning to a final parabolic hardening stage, and exhibit much stronger hardening effects than the binary Fe–26Al alloy despite lower initial yield stresses (Fig. 2b).
evidence_text: The deformation behaviour of Fe–26Al–(2 or 4)M–1C alloys (Fig. 2b) exhibits very high strengthening with almost constant hardening rates, until a final stage of parabolic work hardening is reached. Despite lower yield stresses of most Fe–26Al based alloys in comparison to the binary alloy, all these alloys exhibit much stronger hardening effects during plastic deformation.

claim: Tensile tests exhibit earlier plastic yielding than compression tests (i.e., higher yield stress in compression than in tension), confirming tension–compression asymmetry typical of bcc metals, attributed to nonplanar screw dislocation glide, the Bauschinger effect, and notch sensitivity from precipitates.
evidence_text: The tensile tests of both Fe–15Al-based and Fe–26Al-based alloys revealed an earlier onset of plastic deformation than during compression tests, i.e. the values of yield stresses in compression are higher than those in tension. This phenomenon—the so-called tension–compression asymmetry is known in metals with bcc crystal structure. The tension–compression asymmetry has primarily been related to the glide of the screw dislocations with nonplanar (delocalised) core structure. Another factor contributing to the tension–compression asymmetry could be the Bauschinger effect... Another significant contribution... is related to the notch sensitivity of the matrix due to the presence of precipitates.

claim: Fe–26Al–2V–1C with thin plate-like VC precipitates (≈0.5 mm thickness) shows a pronounced tension–compression asymmetry of approximately 36%.
evidence_text: the alloy Fe–26Al–2V–1C with relatively thin (about 0.5 mm in thickness) and sharp plate-like vanadium carbide precipitates shows a pronounced tension–compression asymmetry (z36%).

claim: Fracture facets in Fe–26Al–2V–1C form on (001) matrix planes parallel to (001) planes of plate-like VC precipitates, indicating the influence of precipitate crystallography on fracture path (Fig. 4i).
evidence_text: The fracture surface of the alloy Fe–26Al–2V–1C with uniform steps indicates transgranular fracture (Fig. 4i), with the influence of the crystallographic orientation of plate-like vanadium carbide precipitates. ... it can be stated that the fracture facets are formed on the (001) planes of the matrix which are parallel to the (001) planes of the precipitates.

claim: Fe–26Al–4Ti–1C fractures almost completely by intergranular decohesion, and cube-like TiC precipitates are visible on intergranular facets (Fig. 4h).
evidence_text: The alloy Fe–26Al–4Ti–1C was fractured almost completely with intergranular decohesion. On the intergranular fracture areas of the alloys Fe–26Al–2Ti–1C and Fe–26Al–4Ti–1C the cube-like TiC precipitates can be clearly distinguished.

claim: Fe–15Al–2Ti–1C exhibits yield stress in compression of 480 MPa at room temperature, 350 MPa at 600 °C, and 100 MPa at 800 °C (Fig. 5).
Fe–15Al–2V–1C has a yield stress in compression of 400 MPa at room temperature, 320 MPa at 600 °C, and 100 MPa at 800 °C (Fig. 5).
Fe–15Al–2Nb–1C exhibits a yield stress in compression of 830 MPa at room temperature, 500 MPa at 600 °C, and 100 MPa at 800 °C (Fig. 5).
Fe–15Al–2Ta–1C has a yield stress in compression of 860 MPa at room temperature, 550 MPa at 600 °C, and 100 MPa at 800 °C (Fig. 5).
Fe-26Al-based alloys with Ti or V additions exhibit a yield stress anomaly with a peak at about 550 °C and a maximum yield stress of about 400 MPa.
evidence_text: The temperature at which the peak occurs is about 550 °C and the yield stress at the peak maximum is about 400 MPa for the alloys with Ti and V (Fig. 6a).

claim: Fe-26Al-4Nb-1C exhibits a yield stress anomaly peak at 474 °C with a maximum yield stress of 619 MPa, the highest among all tested alloys.
evidence_text: The alloy Fe–26Al–4Nb–1C exhibits the most significant shift of the peak position (474 °C, 619 MPa).

claim: At 650°C, all Fe-15Al-based and Fe-26Al-based alloys show tension-compression asymmetry, with compressive yield stress exceeding tensile yield stress.
evidence_text: The comparison of the yield stress in compression and in tension at 650 °C (Fig. 7) as well as at 800 °C (Fig. 8) confirms the asymmetric tension–compression behaviour of all investigated alloys at both temperatures.

claim: At 800°C, all alloys similarly exhibit tension-compression asymmetry.
evidence_text: The comparison of the yield stress in compression and in tension at 800 °C (Fig. 8) confirms the asymmetric tension–compression behaviour of all investigated alloys at both temperatures.

claim: The highest room-temperature ductility values among the investigated alloys are 3.3% for Fe-15Al-2Ti-1C and 1.4% for Fe-26Al-2Nb-1C.
evidence_text: Apart from the binary Fe–15Al alloy, the highest values of room temperature ductility were obtained for the alloys Fe–15Al–2Ti–1C (z3.3%) and Fe–26Al–2Nb–C (z1.4%).
